topics.title: X.509 Certificate
topics.definition: An X.509 certificate is a digitally signed electronic document that binds a subject's identity to a public key. It is issued and signed by a Certificate Authority (CA).
examPoints: An X.509 certificate is a digitally signed electronic document that binds a subject's identity to a public key. It is issued and signed by a Certificate Authority (CA).
Contains Public Key, never Private Key.
Used in HTTPS, VPN, Email, Code Signing, Wi-Fi.
topics.narration:
X.509 Certificate

An X.509 certificate is a digitally signed electronic document that binds a subject's identity to a public key. It is issued and signed by a Certificate Authority (CA).

Important exam points. An X.509 certificate is a digitally signed electronic document that binds a subject's identity to a public key. It is issued and signed by a Certificate Authority (CA).. Contains Public Key, never Private Key.. Used in HTTPS, VPN, Email, Code Signing, Wi-Fi.

topics.title: Certificate Structure
topics.definition: An X.509 certificate consists of three main parts: 1. TBSCertificate 2. Signature Algorithm 3. Digital Signature (Signature Value)
examPoints: An X.509 certificate consists of three main parts:
workflows: Certificate │ ├── TBSCertificate ├── Signature Algorithm └── Signature Value
topics.narration:
Certificate Structure

An X.509 certificate consists of three main parts: 1. TBSCertificate 2. Signature Algorithm 3. Digital Signature (Signature Value)

Important exam points. An X.509 certificate consists of three main parts:

Workflow. Certificate │ ├── TBSCertificate ├── Signature Algorithm └── Signature Value

topics.title: TBSCertificate
topics.definition: TBSCertificate (To Be Signed Certificate) contains all certificate information that the CA signs.
examPoints: TBSCertificate (To Be Signed Certificate) contains all certificate information that the CA signs.
If TBSCertificate changes → Signature becomes invalid.
topics.narration:
TBSCertificate

TBSCertificate (To Be Signed Certificate) contains all certificate information that the CA signs.

Important exam points. TBSCertificate (To Be Signed Certificate) contains all certificate information that the CA signs.. Contains:. Subject. Issuer. Public Key. Serial Number. Validity. Extensions. If TBSCertificate changes → Signature becomes invalid.

topics.title: Signature Algorithm
topics.definition: Specifies the algorithm used by the CA to create the digital signature.
examPoints: Specifies the algorithm used by the CA to create the digital signature.
topics.narration:
Signature Algorithm

Specifies the algorithm used by the CA to create the digital signature.

Important exam points. Specifies the algorithm used by the CA to create the digital signature.. Examples:. SHA256WithRSA. SHA384WithRSA. ECDSA with SHA256

topics.title: Signature Value
topics.definition: The Signature Value is the CA's digital signature over the TBSCertificate.
examPoints: The Signature Value is the CA's digital signature over the TBSCertificate.
topics.narration:
Signature Value

The Signature Value is the CA's digital signature over the TBSCertificate.

Important exam points. The Signature Value is the CA's digital signature over the TBSCertificate.. Created using. CA Private Key. Verified using. CA Public Key

topics.title: ASN.1
topics.definition: ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One) defines how certificate data is structured before encoding.
examPoints: ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One) defines how certificate data is structured before encoding.
topics.narration:
ASN.1

ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One) defines how certificate data is structured before encoding.

Important exam points. ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One) defines how certificate data is structured before encoding.. Encoding Standards. DER. BER

topics.title: DER
topics.definition: DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the binary encoding format of an X.509 certificate.
examPoints: DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the binary encoding format of an X.509 certificate.
topics.narration:
DER

DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the binary encoding format of an X.509 certificate.

Important exam points. DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the binary encoding format of an X.509 certificate.. Binary. Smaller. Machine-readable

topics.title: PEM
topics.definition: PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) is the Base64 encoded text representation of DER data.
examPoints: PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) is the Base64 encoded text representation of DER data.
topics.narration:
PEM

PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) is the Base64 encoded text representation of DER data.

Important exam points. PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) is the Base64 encoded text representation of DER data.. Starts with. Ends with

Workflow. -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----. -----END CERTIFICATE-----

topics.title: DER vs PEM
topics.narration:
DER vs PEM

Comparison. DER: Binary; PEM: Base64 Text. DER: Smaller; PEM: Human Readable. DER: .der .cer; PEM: .pem .crt

topics.title: Certificate Generation
topics.definition: A certificate is created when a CA signs a CSR using its private key.
examPoints: A certificate is created when a CA signs a CSR using its private key.
workflows: Private Key ↓ CSR ↓ CA Signs ↓ Certificate
topics.narration:
Certificate Generation

A certificate is created when a CA signs a CSR using its private key.

topics.title: Certificate Chain
topics.definition: A certificate chain is the ordered sequence of certificates from the server certificate to the trusted Root CA.
examPoints: A certificate chain is the ordered sequence of certificates from the server certificate to the trusted Root CA.
workflows: Root CA ↓ Intermediate CA ↓ Server Certificate
topics.narration:
Certificate Chain

A certificate chain is the ordered sequence of certificates from the server certificate to the trusted Root CA.

topics.title: Validation Steps
workflows: Receive Certificate ↓ Check Signature ↓ Check Issuer ↓ Build Chain ↓ Check Root Trust ↓ Check Validity ↓ Check Revocation ↓ Check Hostname (SAN) ↓ Accept

Validation Steps

Workflow. Receive Certificate ↓ Check Signature ↓ Check Issuer ↓ Build Chain ↓ Check Root Trust ↓ Check Validity ↓ Check Revocation ↓ Check Hostname (SAN) ↓ Accept

topics.title: Top 30 One-Liners for Exam
oneLiners: X.509 is the standard certificate format.
Certificates contain public keys, never private keys.
A certificate has TBSCertificate + Signature Algorithm + Signature Value.
TBSCertificate is the portion signed by the CA.
Changing any TBSCertificate field invalidates the signature.
DER is binary; PEM is Base64 text.
PEM begins with -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----.
Every certificate has a unique serial number.
Issuer is the CA; Subject is the owner.
Not Before and Not After define certificate validity.
DN = Distinguished Name.
DN includes CN, O, OU, C, ST, L.
Root CA certificates are self-signed.
SAN is used by modern browsers for hostname validation.
Browsers ignore CN if SAN is present.
CA:TRUE means the certificate can issue certificates.
CA:FALSE means an end-entity certificate.
KU defines allowed cryptographic operations.
EKU defines intended application.
AKI identifies the issuer's key.
SKI identifies the subject's key.
Certificate chain = Server → Intermediate → Root.
The Root CA is the trust anchor.
Validation checks signature, chain, expiry, revocation, SAN, and KU/EKU.
Missing Intermediate CA often causes "certificate chain incomplete" errors.
Wrong SAN causes hostname mismatch errors.
Unknown Root CA causes untrusted certificate errors.
CRL and OCSP are used for revocation checking.
OpenSSL command to inspect a certificate: openssl x509 -text -noout -in server.crt.
The most commonly tested X.509 extensions are KU, EKU, SAN, Basic Constraints, AKI, and SKI.

AKI identifies the issuer's key.. SKI identifies the subject's key.. Certificate chain = Server → Intermediate → Root.. The Root CA is the trust anchor.. Unknown Root CA causes untrusted certificate errors.. CRL and OCSP are used for revocation checking.. OpenSSL command to inspect a certificate: openssl x509 -text -noout -in server.crt.. The most commonly tested X.509 extensions are KU, EKU, SAN, Basic Constraints, AKI, and SKI.
